findings.title: BotConfig.direction has no validator -- unvalidated value reaches broker order execution
findings.description: POST /api/bots uses BotConfig directly as the request model (bot_manager.py line 72: direction: str = "long"). BotConfig has no @field_validator for direction. An attacker can POST direction="FLAT" or any arbitrary string. The value propagates to: is_short = config.direction == "short" (line 416), compute_realized_pnl() direction filter, journal entries, and the OTO bracket-selection logic. A direction value other than "long" or "short" silently evaluates is_short=False (treated as long), PnL scoping breaks, and journal rows are tagged with an unrecognized direction that compute_realized_pnl() never matches -- causing displayed equity to grow unboundedly as losses are excluded. StrategyRequest gained the validator in F28d, and UpdateBotRequest (routes/bots.py lines 69-74) already had one before this PR. BotConfig is the remaining unprotected model on the create path.
findings.attack_path: POST /api/bots with {"direction": "invalid"} -> BotConfig constructed with no validation -> config.direction == "short" is False -> bot executes long-side orders regardless of intended direction -> PnL journal rows tagged with unrecognized direction -> compute_realized_pnl() direction filter never matches -> displayed capital diverges from actual
findings.fix: Add @field_validator("direction") to BotConfig in bot_manager.py, matching the pattern already in StrategyRequest and UpdateBotRequest.

findings.title: F29 ticker validation does not strip internal whitespace or control characters -- log injection possible
findings.description:
The new validation in quote.py (strip + uppercase + len <= 20) rejects empty and oversized symbols but sym.strip() only removes leading/trailing whitespace. A symbol like "AAPL
INJECTED" passes: strip() leaves the internal newline intact, upper() preserves it, len is still <= 20. This value is passed to _fetch() and logged verbatim via logger.debug (shared.py lines 446/449: "cache HIT %s %s...", ticker). If logs are forwarded to a SIEM or log aggregator, the embedded newline splits the log entry into two lines, allowing an attacker to forge log events. This is not RCE; the ticker is passed to yfinance as an argument, not a shell command. Risk is log integrity only.
findings.attack_path: POST /api/quotes with ["AAPL\nFAKE severity=CRITICAL event=breach"] -> strip() leaves internal \n -> passes len check if total <= 20 -> logged as two lines by structured logger -> SIEM ingests injected record
findings.fix: After strip/upper, validate against a character-class allowlist: re.match(r"^[A-Z0-9./569Xl]{1,20}$", sym). Apply the same fix to the single-ticker GET endpoint at line 12-14.

findings.title: F31: Literal["AND","OR"] annotation on eval_rules is not enforced at runtime -- silent fallback to OR semantics on unexpected values
findings.description: Python does not enforce Literal at runtime. If a logic value other than "AND" or "OR" reaches eval_rules() (e.g. from a manually-edited bots.json before Pydantic parses it, or from a future internal call site that bypasses Pydantic), the function returns any(results) as the fallback (line 469: all(results) if logic == "AND" else any(results)). This silently switches AND-configured strategies to OR logic, potentially causing unexpected trade entries. The bots.json load path IS protected by Pydantic LogicField BeforeValidator, so this requires either a code bug or file tampering to trigger. The annotation provides no runtime protection.
findings.attack_path: Manual tamper of backend/data/bots.json buy_logic field to an unrecognized value -> Pydantic BeforeValidator does v.upper() -> Literal validation rejects it with ValidationError (bot fails to load). Attack is blocked by Pydantic. Risk is residual for future internal callers.
findings.fix: Add an explicit runtime guard at the top of eval_rules: if logic not in ("AND", "OR"): raise ValueError(f"Unknown logic: {logic!r}"). This converts a silent bug into a loud failure.

residual_risks.description: The source query parameter in GET /api/quote/{ticker} and POST /api/quotes is not validated at the route layer. _fetch() rejects unknown values with HTTP 400 via provider dict lookup, so there is no injection risk. However, probing source values leaks which broker backends are configured (400 vs 404/500 error codes). Low risk for a local tool.

Neither StrategyRequest.ticker (models.py line 52) nor BotConfig.symbol (bot_manager.py line 47) have length or character-class validators. Both fields are passed to _fetch() which logs them. The backtest and bot create paths have the same log-injection exposure as SEC-02, but are lower priority because those endpoints are presumably authenticated or internal.

testing_gaps.description: No test verifies that POST /api/bots with direction="invalid" is rejected. The test suite covers direction="short" and direction="long" in backtest scenarios, but BotConfig validation (SEC-01) has no coverage.
testing_gaps.suggested_test: Assert BotConfig(direction="SIDEWAYS", ...) raises ValidationError; assert POST /api/bots with direction="garbage" returns HTTP 422.

testing_gaps.description: No test for POST /api/quotes with symbols containing internal newlines or control characters (SEC-02).
testing_gaps.suggested_test: Assert POST /api/quotes with ["AAPL\nINJECTED"] returns price=null or HTTP 400, not a successful cache entry with the injected ticker key.
